0. A little bit of History
0. A little bit of History: Before 2023 (last century)
Decades ago, scientists started to imagine and define what agent should look like in the field of AI.

In 1995, Russell and Norvig said “agents operate autonomously, perceive their environments and persist over a prolonged time period, adapt to change and create and pursue goals” in the book Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach (first edition).
People in different fields, such as robotics, figured out what sort of agents they would like to research (not our focus though).

Scale things up: Craig Reynolds developed “boids” in 1986, showing that simple agent rules can produce global emergent behavior. This established the idea that societies of agents can produce intelligence exceeding the sum of their parts.

0. A little bit of Review of Presentation 1: ReAct

ReAct architecture requires LLMs to output tool messages in a specific format to call tools. And then, these tool messages would be parsed and sent to functions for execution. Finally, these tools would return outcomes (Observations) back to LLM.
Notably, ability of calling tools is also obtained through post-training.
0. A little bit of Review of Presentation 1: A nice chart
Figure 1: LangChain illustration of ReAct
Input goes into LLM.
LLM might or might not reason, then would or would not take actions (call tools).
If tool is called, observation would be returned to inform reasoning or next action.
Then steps above repeat until LLM thinks it’s finished. Then output is returned to user.
This is the biggest takeaway you need from this tutorial. I hope you won’t forget this after you graduate!!!

1. How a LLM is trained from scratch?: Pre-Training
Initially, a LLM is randomly initialized. It could do nothing but output nonsense, which makes sense.

Then, developers want to train it using QA pairs, which is what we think when dealing with other models. But human being don’t have that many high-quality QA pairs. And hiring someone to make QA pairs is prohibitively expensive.
So, developers came up with an idea that LLM learns everything on its own first through learning how to predict next token (pretext task).
(To learn more about pretext task, please refer to self-supervised learning.)
Then, LLM could auto-complete prompts. At this moment, this is called base model.
(When you are downloading LLMs from HuggingFace, if the name of LLM doesn’t contain “instruct”, it’s base model by default, except gpt-oss-20b and 120b. If it contains “base”, it’s base model.)

0. Common Sense & A Quick Review of QuickStart
0. A Quick Review of QuickStart: What? I need to install packages?

You made a Python package that help you make use of AI to make AI Agents whenever you want. Let’s call it ai-agents-factory. And obviously, native Python doesn’t have this functionality, and you also want others to use. Then you build the wheel and publish it.
This Python package would be available in PyPi, which is maintained by Python Software Foundation, the organization behind Python language.
Of course, if what you publish contains virus, it would be removed and nobody can search it through pip or any other python package managers.
0. A Quick Review of QuickStart: What? I need to install packages?

You keep updating it and publish multiple versions (releases management). One day, you found the following feature from Python 3.10 useful then you decide to use it in newer version of your Python package. Let’s say 2.0.

Then, regardless of what Python package manager you are using, uv or native pip, as long as you are using Python <3.10, ai-agents-factory 2.0 would NEVER be indexed and downloaded. This is why some of you can not download new langchain (1.2.x+). If you are using Python <3.11, old langchain would be indexed and downloaded.
Old langchain is not the focus of LangChain Inc but it’s still being maintained.
Btw we are learning new langchain, as stated in Presentation 1.
One of the reasons that I don’t like colab is because it has a lot of packages pre-installed and you might never realize you need to install additional packages.
0. A Quick Review of QuickStart: Environment Variables

I am not teaching computer literacy but just to let you know that in windows OS, environment variables are stored like key-value pairs. The OPENAI_API_KEY is the variable, the sk-………. in the email I sent you is the value.

You could add OPENAI_API_KEY in it for sure but to make changes take effect, you need to restart your machine, which is a little bit inconvenient. If you do that, it could be really convenient when programming at some point because under the hood, openai, the python package would try to find an environment variable called “OPENAI_API_KEY” and retrieve its value. But when you need to update your key, you might have to restart your machine.
This is why I told you to use .env file, which is more flexible. Updating API key doesn’t need to restart anything.
0. A Quick Review of QuickStart: API vs API key
One day, Michael deployed a home-made LLM in a server with public IP address. He wants to sell this LLM. So he made a Python package and named it “michael-llm”.
Anyone who downloaded this package meets the technical prereq to use michael’s LLM.
But Michael doesn’t want everyone to use. Then he sent some keys to those who paid for this service so that only the key holders could access his LLM.
We call the key “API key”. The functions you use to access michael’s LLM are called “API”.

1.1.1 Core Components of LangChain (without Middleware): Models: Init, Invocation and Runtime-Configurable Models
Recall:
After you initialize a LLM successfully, you can send something as input to this LLM, which is called “invocation”.
You can invoke the LLM with multiple independent requests in parallel as well, which is called “batch”.
You could make invocation/batch interactive to improve user experience, which is called “stream”.

If you want to decide what kind of model to invoke, what temperature to use and so on at invocation, rather than initialization, you can use parameter configurable_fields and specify what could be determined dynamically.
1.1.1 Core Components of LangChain (without Middleware): Models: Init, Invocation and Runtime-Configurable Models

There are 4 types of Messages in LangChain but most of the time you would use 3 of them. SystemMessage is system prompt and HumanMessage is user prompt, which is what you typed in to LLM when playing with ChatGPT. AIMessage means LLM’s output.
You could do it in several ways, dict or using their pre-defined classes. They serve the same purpose.
If you don’t specify anything and just throw a sentence to invoke function, it would be considered as user prompt.
You might use AIMessage in invoke function sometimes for one-shot prompting or few-shot prompting (What’s attached below is GPT-3 paper that tells you few-shot prompting impact. FYI).

2. Hands-On Project: Travel Plan after Retirement
We would do a fun project with Google Maps API key!!

Project Description: Michael Zhang retired and he would like to travel around the world. He needs a Python program to do trips planning for him, which is what agent is good at accidentally. He also loves passing by multiple cities a time. For example, he might go to Toronto first and stay there for couple days, then go to New York, finally Miami.

He wants a program that could accept multiple city names and period of time of stay for each city and could output a detailed multi-city travel plan. Since LLM can’t predict weather but Google Maps API could, you are expected to build single agent application using LangChain framework that could use tools to query weather and others.
Michael might have question for generated travel plan so short-term memory must be enabled otherwise Michael would be mad!
Michael would like to know what cloths to wear in each city and whether he should use umbrella so you must use google weather API to forecast weather!

He cares about air quality and would like to know how many masks he needs in total (if a city’s air quality is bad, Michael would put on a mask. But what’s bad is up to you when developing python program as long as you don’t make it ridiculous).

1. Think in Graph: Let’s chain these parrots together

Still remember the interesting explanation of LangChain logo? Unfortunately, LangChain, the Python package, alone, can’t achieve what LangChain logo means to do. LangGraph is the one you would use to chain these parrots (LLMs) together.
What are the differences between LangChain and LangGraph? Why LangGraph is a separate package, rather than a part of LangChain?
1. Think in Graph: Let’s think of problem step by step
When we face a problem, what’s the commonly-used pattern of thinking of solution?
For example, how to make a bowl of salad?
First, you might buy the vegetables and eggs and sauces. Second, you boil the eggs. Third, you would cut the vegetables…
You solve the problem STEP BY STEP.
In LangGraph, the step we talked about here is a node. An edge would decide what’s the next node to call.
The state = data structure (schema) + reducer (might not talk about it in this lecture), simply.
Designing multi-agent is nothing but designing a Python script to solve a problem step by step, with some logics and engineering details added, using LangGraph.
A node is basically a function and an edge is a bunch of if else bundled to decide what’s next.
If something could be done by plain LLM or agent in this node, you can simply call it multi-agent.
Actually, you could put whatever you like in a node.

3. Super Step
This workflow works in parallel after START node receives input.
For example, if the custom state has only one field “user_input” and the input is {“user_input”: “start: ”}
Then, what node_a does is to append “a” to the “user_input” field of state. Similarly, node_b would update state by appending “b” to “user_input” of state.
What node_c would see after it receives state from node_b?
A. {“user_input”: “start: a”}
B. {“user_input”: “start: b”}
C. {“user_input”: “start: ab”} or {“user_input”: “start: ba”}

Day 2
Recap of Day 1
Data analytics is the non-trivial process of identifying valid, novel, potentially useful, and ultimately understandable patterns in data stored in structured databases
Data mining is a PROCESS not a tool or formula.
It starts with identifying what the business issue is and how data may be able to inform the decision.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Objectives for Day 2
Problem finding and translating a business issue into a data problem
Where do we get data and what does it look like?
What is the “quality” of the data?
How do we prepare the data for analysis using Excel?
MGSC 1207 Fall 2025 Day 3 Data Understanding
Good data analytics is good storytelling.
There are many many decisions that are made about the data, even before the analysis begins.
Few people even think about these decisions and don’t respect this preparatory work.
But you have to be ready for those that are thinking and asking these questions.
Being able to respond to these questions is critical to ensuring your audience believes what you discover.
Often, you can tell your story is a single picture.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
An example:
COVID has had a devastating impact on mortality in Canada
- but is this really true?
How do you know a death was due to COVID?
Most are old people who are going to die anyway.
There is variation in mortality year to year.  This is one of the up years and next year it will go down again.
This is a hoax – fake news!
What has mortality data looked like during the pandemic?

MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
OK, but what is this supposed to show me?  Mortality rates drop in summer?

With a smaller population, weekly mortality rates are more volatile.
We did very well at the start of the pandemic, but since the arrival of Omicron and the lifting of restrictions, mortality rates have increased substantially.
But there is no longer the public support to re-introduce mandates – the model may say one thing, but it is important to understand all the actors for effective implementation.
But 2023 looks good – fewer deaths than expected!  But maybe those that would have died in 2023, died in 2022!!
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
A simple picture can tell a powerful story.
Much thought has to go into
what you are trying to say,
how to measure it,
how to “clean” the data,
how to say it as simply as possible.
Previous charts are from the DailyStats blog of the NS Department of Finance.  They did not update the charts in 2024.
The hard work is often boring.

Business Understanding
Often the organization has difficulty articulating their issue/problem.
Example:
You are asked to prepare a report for a senior university administrator.
Students feel that the administration needs to understand how finances are affecting students.
But what does this mean?  What is the issue?
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
A question might be:
“To what extent is the increasing cost of university education, high rents and limited student income affecting student academic and personal success and health?”
The question puts some boundaries on what is in and not in the report.
It may also be seen as “biased” because attention is drawn to costs increasing and student income being limited.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
The report should be structured around answering very specific questions.
What would be good questions that would speak clearly to the problem you have defined?
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Report might focus on answering specific questions:
What are sources of student income/financial resources?
How does income vary among student populations?
Who is working while at school and how much?
Does working affect mental health?
Who is stressed about paying for school?
How is working and/or stress affecting grades?
Many questions may need to be asked before you can define a problem that we can analyze.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Data Understanding
Where can we get data?
Administrative databases (e.g., Banner has student records)
3rd party sources (Statistics  Canada, purchased data,…)
a course survey?
Regrettably, few groups are looking seriously at this issue.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Samples and Populations
A population is “everybody” whereas the sample is who you have data about.
You may wish to talk about consumers, but you only have data about those who have purchased your service/product.
Ideally we want to talk about a general population, but this can only be done if our sample is representative of the population.
The only way to ensure your sample is representative is if it is a “random” sample.
To be a random sample, everyone in the population must have equal chance of being included in your sample.  An example of this is seen later when we introduce “experiments”.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Administrative data
Structured data databases – customer data, transaction data, supplier data, …
Databases are complex interconnected files (relational database)
Amazon has records about me.
In one file they have the various addresses that I have shipped to.
In another they have the various credit cards I have used.
In another there are the many purchase transactions.
In another there is the history of what items I have viewed and when, how long,…
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Extract, Transform, Load  (ETL)
To do analysis, we need to extract the data we want and organize it in a fashion we can easily analyze.
Usually this means copying data into a “flat file” that looks like a simple spreadsheet.
Each row is one “observation” (record) and the columns are the attributes (variables).
If we were studying customers, then ideally we would want one row (record) for each unique customer.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Since I have made many visits and made many purchases, I might have to transform the raw information into new variables, such as,
Average number of visits per month;
Average purchase in $$;
Average number of items purchased per order;
Where I live (billing address?).
When we have the data is the desired form, we will load it for analysis.
We want a FLAT file.  Each column represents a variable.  Each row is an observation.

You work with what you have and explore how you can address what is missing.
The resulting data file may be a combination of data from multiple sources that have been merged.  Easy?  Not!  Can present unintended problems.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Surveys
To capture data not available in the administrative databases, you may try doing a survey.
You invite individuals to answer a variety of questions

In previous terms, students in MGSC 1207 were asked how much they earned and saved in the summer, how much they were paying in tuition, whether they were worried about paying for school, what was their GPA, had they ever failed a course, where were they from,….
Some questions corresponded to what might have been in Banner, but many did not.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
What if we wanted to merge the survey and administrative data sets
What issues might we encounter?
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Missing data
Privacy/permission
How do you link anonymous survey data with personal administrative data?
What if the data is different?
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Challenges with data
Only 75% of students participated in the 2019-2024 surveys.
Many online surveys are open to an unlimited number of people.  Maybe 1,000 respond but that might be less than 0.1% of those who could have.

Surveys are an example of an “observational study” – you only have data on those you have data for.  What about those who did not respond?  Would their responses have been similar?  Don’t know since they didn’t tell you.

Administrative data is also observational.  In studying student retention and persistence, we only have data on those students who enrolled at Saint Mary’s.  What about those who applied but did not enroll?  What about those that never applied?
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Experiments vs Observational Studies
How can we control for some challenges that come with an observational study?
In online marketing, they often do A/B experiments.
When you visit a webpage, you may see a different advertisement than another visitor.
Visitors are randomly shown ad A or ad B.
Your response is tracked and the firm evaluates which ad generates a more positive response.
With experiments you can exclude all other factors that may affect behavior so you can be confident in drawing conclusions that can be generalized to the general population.
Experiments control for factors that allow you to generalize.
Unfortunately, most of the time you cannot randomly assign “treatments” and you must work with observational data.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Data Quality
Data quality may be more important than the volume of data.
What is data quality?
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding

In 2010, students were asked how much they earned last summer.  How much did you earn?  Do you remember?  Most gave numbers to the nearest $1,000 but one said $9,655.  How can he be so precise?  One said $650,025!
Some cannot remember with precision
Some will make data entry errors
Some are not honest
Some will misunderstand the question
The problem is not just with surveys.  There can be errors in administrative data.
Many fall students show as transfer students on Banner, but when I look at the transcript, there are no transfer credits.  Was no credit given or are they not transfers?
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Background
Universities and colleges around the world partner with Studiosity to provide academic writing and core skills support 24/7, connecting students to help when they need it.
They currently work with 75% of universities in Australia, 21 universities in the United Kingdom, and have recently started providing support to Canadian institutions.
As of 2022, Studiosity’s services are available worldwide to over 1.6 million students.
2022 Canadian Student Wellbeing Survey
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Research objectives

This annual survey seeks to better understand the motivations, emotions, and demands of postsecondary students in Canada, and how to better tailor initiatives and solutions. The survey investigates key areas of student wellbeing.

In 2022, the survey focused on topics such as experiences of stress, importance of grades, motivation and, cheating, feelings towards the future, intent to withdraw, engagement with institution, and the transition to postsecondary.

In addition, the results were benchmarked with the 2021 survey to better understand how behaviours and attitudes have changed and how these themes have shifted after another year of the COVID-19 pandemic.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Methodology & Sample
Studiosity produced the questions for this survey, and Angus Reid Forum gathered the responses from current Canadian postsecondary students via an online survey.
The survey ran from March 10 to March 24, 2022 and gained a total of 1,014 responses.

The sample frame was balanced to ensure representation of men and women in proportion to their overall share of the Canadian postsecondary student population (56% female, 43% male), as well as to ensure statistically significant representation from different regions of the country.
The sample was comprised of 75% full-time students and 25% parttime students, and was conducted in English and French.
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
What are your thoughts?  How did they obtain their sample?
Who is the population?
Do you think the sample frame aligns well with the population?
Who was in the sample frame?  Who had the opportunity to participate?
What cautions might you have about accepting the survey results?
MBAN 5520 Day 2 Fall 2025 Data Understanding
Data quality questions to ask

Data Preparation best practices
Always protect the original data from damage.  Select only the variables you think you may need and put them in your working data file.
Make the data talk to you in language you understand:
Give variables meaningful names.
Recode values with understandable values.
Remove unnecessary detail by grouping values if appropriate.
Make note of anything unusual.  Such cases should be investigated.

Keep track of what you have done and keep these notes with your working data file (e.g., keep a data dictionary, keep all your look up tables used for recoding, …).  It is easy to forget what changes you have made.

The objective for grouping data is to
Make the summary concise

MBAN 5520 Day 3 Fall 2024
What does this mean?  How do we do it well?
Don’t have too many groups – not concise
Don’t have too few groups – loss of information
Groups should be unambiguous (should not overlap so each value fits in only one group)
The summary should include all the data
Use “nice numbers.  We like multiples of 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100 and not starting or ending values like 13.7

It is easier to compare groups that are of “equal size”.  Our groups went in increments of $20,000.  But if “very high” had a range of $40,000 ($80,000 to $120,000), then that would not be good practice.
To satisfy all of the above, the 1st and/or last group may need to be larger to capture outliers.  E.g., our last group is $100,000 and above.

Mean versus Median
The median is unaffected by outliers.
The median is unaffected by any especially large or small values – just the values exactly in the middle.

MBAN 5520 Day 3 Fall 2025
Empirical Rule
If the distribution of your data is bell shaped (like the Normal distribution), then
Approximately 68% of the values should be between
Mean – StdDev  and Mean + StdDev
Approximately 95% of the values should be between
Mean – 2*StdDev  and  Mean + 2*StdDev
And almost no values should be more than 3 StdDev from the Mean.

The rule only works well when the distribution is reasonably symmetrical with no outliers.
It is good when you are only looking at common (typical) values.
The Empirical Rule is a quick and dirty way of defining
Low/High as 1 StdDev below or above the mean and
Very Low/High as 2 StdDev below or above the mean
The Empirical Rule generally works if the distribution is not severely skewed.

Creating the charts/tables is easy.

The percentages we calculate are estimates.
If you were to survey a different group of students you would get slightly different results.
How different?
The variability in our estimates can be described by their standard errors.
As suggested by the Empirical Rule, do not be surprised if your estimates are off by one standard error but it is unusual to be off by more than two standard errors.
How large can the standard error be?  It depends upon the sample size.
Sample Size
Max Standard Error
Unless our sample is reasonably large, there could be significant error.

Summary
Pivot Tables can effectively summarize frequency data, both numeric and categorical
Scale is important – look at percentages rather than frequencies
Keep tables simple – remove unnecessary digits and categories
Sequence categories logically
Charts are often more effective than tables
Percentage of Row Total or Column Total?  The choice is important.
Comparing three variables can be confusing.
Beware of small samples.
Estimates can be unreliable with small samples.
MBAN 5520 Day 3 Fall 2025
Simpson’s Paradox
Recall when comparing salary expectations of Males and Females, we observed that Males expected to earn more than Females.
Is the difference due to gender or another “lurking” variable?
We know that Females are more likely to study Arts.
We know that Arts grads expect to earn less because their studies are less career focused.
Is the gender difference really a program difference?
In this case we found that the gender difference existed for all programs.  There was both a program difference and a gender difference.
But what if program was the explanation?
MBAN 5520 Day 3 Fall 2025
Aggregating Data and Lurking Variables
All data is aggregated.
We can always split our sample into smaller subsets by examining an additional variable.
We split Females by Program.
We could split Female Arts students by where they are from or their year of study.
Differences we see at one level may be explained by an additional, “lurking”, variable.
The better we know our subject of study and our data, the more likely we can identify alternate explanations for patterns (if there really is an alternate explanation).

Think twice before jumping to conclusions when you discover a difference.
Ask whether there is an additional factor that may explain the pattern.

Do not automatically accept an alternate explanation.  Look at the data and see if the alternate explanation is really supported. (e.g., maybe Program choice explains gender differences in Salary, but the data did not support this explanation.)
It is human behavior to jump to conclusions when the pattern supports your beliefs.
It is also human behavior to dismiss patterns that do not support your beliefs.

slides.title: Topic One
bullets: What are biases? + Understanding misinference & motivated reasoning
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Cognitive, data & algorithmic biases
bullets: I’ll follow the broad classification used in Ferrara (2024)
Data can obviously carry other biases, e.g. cultural
Similarly, algorithms can potentially be derived that inherit other biases e.g. cultural
Whenever pairs (or more!) of biases intersect the result can be particularly troublesome
Because cognitive biases are so prevalent we’ll focus on those first and come back to data and algorithmic issues
MBAN 5800

slides.title: System 1/System 2 theory
bullets: We don’t have time to delve into detailed neuroscience, but one useful theory is Kahneman’s (2011) Sys1/Sys2 distinction
System 1 – rapid non-cognitive processing
System 2 – slower cognitive, logically reasoned processing
Note attempts to focus these processes to given parts of the brain are disputed
e.g. Sys1 is amygdala, Sys2 is prefontal cortex
However, as a cognitive model it is broadly viewed as being highly useful
But sometimes cast as Sys1 is biased while Sys2 overcomes biases – we’ll see that isn’t accurate
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Heuristics: evolutionary background
bullets: Once a large brain volume had been achieved it still took 7 million years for the homo sapien brain to evolve
Large-scale civilization exists within a ~10,000 year window
The arrival of instantaneous access to information occurred in the last 30 years (and is not evenly distributed)
E.O. Wilson’s summary:
“We have Paleolithic emotions…
medieval institutions….
and godlike technology.”
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Challenges with biases
bullets: For millenia our survival has depended on creating intuitive & rapid assessment frameworks
Easier in a predictable world, harder in a more complex and random world
Arguably farming shifted this emphasis, but that is still less than 15,000 years ago
So assessments that offered advantages (e.g. rapid, or reduced information context) were selected
Need to act fast
Too much information
Storing and adjusting key memories
MBAN 5800
PRESENTATION TITLE
From wikipedia

slides.title: The Edmans (2024) ladder of misinference
bullets: MBAN 5800

slides.title: The ladder of misinference: statements
bullets: Statements are not facts
A statement can misrepresent valid findings (e.g. dropping information, or presenting without context)
Verification of a statement is usually necessary
Is existing evidence quoted (or made-up)
Is the statement consistent with the evidence
Statements from authorities may carry more weight, but ultimately should still be subject to analysis
Even if a statement accurately portrays a study (for example) the study itself can still be wrong
MBAN 5800

slides.title: The ladder of misinference: facts
bullets: A fact about a single issue is not data, representation matters
Even multiple facts collected to construe data may not be, since they may not be representative (e.g. cherry picking)
This easily morphs into narrative fallacy
We interpret cause/effect where there is none
Especially powerful if pairs of biases coincide
Built to Last, by Collins and Porras (1994) found nine common issues among 18 successful companies based on “facts”
Almost half the companies focused suffered considerable fiscal problems over the next decade
MBAN 5800

slides.title: The ladder of misinference: data
bullets: Data is not evidence because it may have been mined
One test out of many being chosen for example
Even if there appears to be a correlation, it can still be spurious
Even when it isn’t spurious, correlations between input and output may have a common cause
e.g. a mother’s IQ may increase the likelihood of breastfeeding and the child having a higher IQ, rather than breastfeeding alone improving IQ
MBAN 5800

slides.title: The ladder of misinference: when is data evidence?
bullets: When there is a control or way to affect the input without impacting the output
e.g. randomized controlled trials
An instrument should not impact the output directly except through it’s influence on the input

Consider the use of rivers in urban areas in educational assessment. Historically these have set the bounds of school boards, lots of rivers means lots of school boards. We don’t anticipate rivers impacting child learning directly.  Therefore predict # school districts based on rivers, and then regress against school performance to see whether more choice improves child performance.
MBAN 5800

slides.title: The ladder of misinference: evidence isn’t always proof
bullets: Even if you go to great lengths with a control, your study may still be limited in applicability i.e. it is not universal
A system may be granular, e.g. what works in one profession does not work in another
The input may be moderated
e.g. 2 litres per hour is better than 1 litre hour doesn’t mean 3 litres is always better than 2
The controls may be moderated

Classic example is West Point grads correlating with mental “grit” over fitness. In fact almost all West Point cadets are very fit, that doesn’t mean the public should prioritize mental toughness over physical fitness.
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Motivated reasoning: why education doesn’t bring agreement
bullets: Motivated reasoning: an individual will seek an argument to counter something they disagree with or don’t understand
Extreme example: “bladder effect” on Deepwater Horizon
Faced with unclear pressure readings they constructed a fatally wrong explanation
The more educated an individual, the more possible routes of argument are available
AI sycophancy makes this potentially highly problematic
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Motivated reasoning: influencing factors (not exhaustive)
bullets: Group/cultural cognition
Can’t change opinion – perceived as disloyalty
Emotional comfort and fear avoidance
Reason towards positive beliefs/away from negative
Cognitive dissonance reduction
Simply doesn’t sit with our belief set
External incentives
Social or monetary rewards
Confirmation bias – important enough we’ll leave to a separate discussion
MBAN 5800

bullets: MBAN 5800

Remember individual responses to various biases will differ
e.g. those who suffer from imposter syndrome will find fault in themselves over others
Those who feel shame about their background/culture (for whatever reason) may disavow their own cultural values
The coincidence of two biases can make something seem even more appealing

slides.title: Confirmation bias:
bullets: Incredibly hard to shake – we are hardwired to accept claims we want to be true (it’s an interpretive bias)
e.g. Theranos, Belle Gibson hoax and app
Creates an asymmetric response around new information
We except favoured views uncritically and are critical of divergent positions
Neuroscience suggests this is down to fast/reflexive thinking over-riding slow/deliberate
Amygdala vs prefontal cortex (e.g. Kaplan, Gimbel & Harris 2016)
Recall Sys 1 vs Sys 2
Mitigation requires conscious seeking of alternatives
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Confirmation bias: just add search
bullets: The ability to easily search for information to support our viewpoints is comparatively new in society
Layers entirely new level of capacity to support our biases
Social and search platforms are algorithmically driven to provide information attuned to your biases
Depending on the platform, you likely will have to seek alternative perspectives
This leads to epistemic isolation and ultimately attitude polarization
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Cultural/framing bias – interlinked issues
bullets: The tendency to interpret through the lens of one’s own culture, values, and societal norms
Can lead to a perception of one’s societal norms being “universal” or “normal”
May lead to narratives around what is considered logical/moral/reasonable
Mitigation: encourage humility, avoid universality
Framing bias occurs when the way information is presented over-rides the information
e.g. 90% success vs 10% failure (consider vaccine context)
Widely discussed in terms of global warming: e.g. reach fiscal conservatives by emphasizing potential financial losses
Even some words imposes frames: students as products, students as consumers, through to students as clients
Mitigation: reconsider with different perspective, or absolutes vs relative
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Anchoring bias
bullets: We tend to be heavily influenced by the first piece (“the anchor”) of information provided on a topic
Example: (current rent $1200) “We’re upping the average rent to $1500, but I’ve decided to give you a break at $1400”
Mitigation: you must cognitively overrule by considering other assessments
It’s dangerous because it distorts later discussion
Especially relevant in teaching!
The anchor can also become a target for manipulation
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Survivorship bias
bullets: Logical error of focusing only on things which “survive” a process (or by extension we consider only winners) – sampling bias
Especially relevant in investing – look at surviving funds on a 3,5,10 year basis
Danger: biased data due to missing information, tendency to elevate strategies that may have failed in missing context
Mitigation: actively look for the missing data, change your focus to be broader
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Cherry picking & anecdotal bias
bullets: Cherry picking (selective evidence bias) involves selecting only things that support a viewpoint – behavioural bias
Intensional exclusion of other data, common in politics, media
Closely allied with confirmation bias
Anecdotal bias: being influenced by a story over statistical evidence
Brain is more likely to remember emotionally interlinked story than facts and figures
Distorts risk perception in many cases
Mitigation for both is to ask what data is not being considered?
You may need to confront personal biases in this step
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Binary thinking/“Black and white” bias
bullets: Cognitive heuristic where we reduce many possible outcomes to two possibilities
e.g. “You’re either with us or against us.”
Problematic because we are prone to seek certainty (uncertainty takes effort to manage)
Encourages stereotyping, moral absolutism
Oversimplifies complex problems
Ignores uncertainty, spectrum of possibilities
Yet another bias that fuels polarization
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Availability/Recency bias
bullets: Those things that are easily recalled (popular or vivid) tend to be given more weight
e.g. after seeing plane crash on news we are inclined not to fly
Brain actively prioritizes this kind of information
Recency bias is a subset: we are more influenced by recent information
Especially relevant in investment theory
People tend to invest on market rise and not when markets fall, while investing theory suggests your returns will be higher for the opposite behaviour
Mitigation: Consider the full data, allow emotion to settle before a decision
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Sunk cost fallacy/too big to fail
bullets: Prior extensive investment in something makes us likely to continue to with it when no longer rational
Rooted in perceived loss aversion (reality: the loss is real)
Emotional attachment and concerns about regret also feature
“Too big to fail” adds additional systemic pressures
Make it even harder to avoid continuing
Introduces major moral hazard: can take bigger risks if rescue is expected
Mitigation: preset exit rules, use external review, normalize resets
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Change aversion/promotion bias
bullets: A tendency to not want to change as the status quo feels safer – cognitive bias
Fear of change is rooted in uncertainty being cognitively challenging
Can be accompanied by resistance to learning new methodology
Losses due to change may feel more significant than potential gains
Change promotion bias is rooted in the belief that all innovation is good
This is arguably an optimism/overconfidence bias
Distinct from the aversion situation
Mitigation: Detail benefits carefully, and properly evaluate impacts
MBAN 5800

bullets: How AI systems can inherit and impose biases

slides.title: Ferrara (2024) outline
bullets: Document makes a distinction between fairness and bias
Fairness is intentional, a desire to avoid biased outcomes
Although a precise definition of “fair” is still lacking…
Bias is not necessarily intentional and can be subconscious
Aside from incorrect outcomes, bias presents legal/regulatory problems
Bias obviously a major issue in terms of public trust
Especially relevant if bias is perceived as deliberate
Hence focus on fairness as an alternative perspective

slides.title: Data biases
bullets: This is arguably the most fundamental bias in AI
Recall what we said in Week 1 about the challenges of using datums out of context
Bias in training data will likely become bias in outcomes
e.g. sampling, representation, measurement biases
Facial recognition example:
Buolamwini and Gebru (2018) showed that because black faces are under-represented in sample data, algorithm performs poorly on that group. Solution is to oversample.

Description
Example

slides.title: Mitigations
bullets: In the pre-training context:
Ensuring datasets are representative of the whole population, including marginalized groups.

slides.title: Algorithmic biases
bullets: It’s not obvious how we separate biases in training vs biases inherent in broader algorithm design
I’m going to include both in the algorithmic context but we could put training issues into data biases
Algorithms can be biased due to biased design choices
e.g. loan decision choices based on geographic default rates
Can be difficult to detect

slides.title: Sources of algorithmic bias
bullets: Source of Bias
Explanation
Optimization/utility function choice
Models optimize for accuracy or efficiency—not fairness—leading to unequal outcomes.
Incorrect assumptions
Many models assume training and deployment environments are identical, which is often untrue.
Feature weighting and decision thresholds
Certain features (e.g. postal code) disproportionately affect predictions for specific groups.
Fairness–accuracy trade-offs
Algorithms may treat fairness as secondary to performance.
Feedback loops
Decisions made by the algorithm influence future data, reinforcing bias (e.g. crime predictions).
MBAN 5800

slides.title: Mitigations I
bullets: In-processing/model selection first:
Choose(build) models that prioritize fairness
Choose classifiers (stat groupings) that enforce demographic parity
e.g. Kamiran and Calders (2012)
Consider ensemble models that combine outputs to reduce bias
Post-processing solutions after training is possible, but considerably trickier
e.g. Yan, S. et al (2020) “Fair class balancing: Enhancing model fairness without observing sensitive attributes.”

slides.title: Mitigations II
bullets: Broader philosophies on design:
Design models with group fairness or individual fairness criteria (e.g. equalized odds, demographic parity).
Transparency and explainability are essential for detecting algorithmic bias.
Continuous auditing, monitoring, and feedback loops, especially for generative AI.
Interdisciplinary teams and ethical governance to guide model decisions.

bullets: It would be amazing to have AI systems that are/have
Transparent to users in terms of decision process, at very least the process is visible to users
Accountable in terms of developers being held responsible for the algorithmic behaviour
A decision process that is explainable
Demonstratable fairness on either individual or group basis

slides.title: Artificial Neural Network
bullets: Basics

slides.title: ANN and Brian
bullets: ANN are computational models inspired by the structure and function of biological neurons in the brain.
To understand how ANN is related to neurons in the brain, we need to discuss the basic components of both biological neurons and artificial neurons.

slides.title: Biological Neurons
bullets: A biological neuron is the basic unit of the nervous system, responsible for processing and transmitting information.
It consists of three main parts:
the cell body (soma),
dendrites,
and the axon.
Information is received by the dendrites, integrated in the cell body, and transmitted via the axon to other neurons through synapses.
The information transfer occurs using electrical and chemical signals.

slides.title: Similarities
bullets: Structure: ANN is composed of layers of artificial neurons, inspired by the organization of biological neurons in the brain. Each layer may consist of numerous artificial neurons, just as the brain contains billions of interconnected biological neurons.

Connection and Communication: In ANN, artificial neurons connect to others through weighted connections, similar to how biological neurons connect through synapses. The strength of these connections represents the weights in ANN, while in the brain, it represents the efficacy of the synapses.

Learning: Both biological neurons and ANN learn from experience. In the brain, learning occurs through changes in synaptic strength, following a process called synaptic plasticity. In ANN, learning is achieved through adjusting the weights of the connections using algorithms like backpropagation.

bullets: The get the best ANN, we need to find the set of parameters that minimize the cost function.
Analytical solutions are not efficient
We use algorithmic approach: Gradient Descent (GD)
ANN uses more efficient GD: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)

bullets: Cost function

slides.title: Is GD efficient?
bullets: Say we have 10,000 data points and 10 features.
The sum of squared residuals consists of as many terms as there are data points, so 10000 terms in our case.
We need to compute the derivative of this function with respect to each of the features, so in effect we will be doing 10000 * 10 = 100,000 computations per iteration.
It is common to take 1000 iterations, in effect we have 100,000 * 1000 = 100000000 computations to complete the algorithm.
That is pretty much an overhead and hence gradient descent is slow on huge data.

slides.title: Learning rate?
bullets: Yes, the main question is how do we find out what the learning rate should be?
A general suggestion is to keep it small and tune it within the training process.
Obviously, we can have an adaptive learning rate that changes at each iteration depending on the change in the MSE.

slides.title: Adjustable Learning Rate
bullets: There are various learning rate scheduler strategies:
Exponential decay: The learning rate is multiplied by a fixed decay rate at each iteration or epoch, as demonstrated in the previous example.
Step decay: The learning rate is reduced by a fixed factor at specific intervals, such as every N epochs. For example, the learning rate could be reduced by a factor of 0.5 every 10 epochs.

Time-based decay: The learning rate is reduced according to a function of the elapsed training time or the number of iterations. For example, the learning rate could be reduced by a factor proportional to the inverse of the square root of the number of iterations.

Cosine annealing: The learning rate is reduced following a cosine function, which allows for periodic “restarts” of the learning rate, helping the optimization process escape local minima or saddle points.
Cyclic learning rates: The learning rate is varied cyclically within a predefined range, allowing the model to explore different areas of the loss surface more effectively.

Adaptive learning rates: These learning rate schedulers adjust the learning rate based on the progress of the optimization process, such as the improvement in the loss function or the validation accuracy. Some well-known adaptive learning rate methods include AdaGrad, RMSprop, and Adam.

slides.title: Efficient GD Algorithms for ANN
bullets: There are three main types of gradient descent:

Batch Gradient Descent: In batch gradient descent, the gradient is computed using the entire dataset. This means that we calculate the average gradient for all the samples in the dataset and then update the model parameters accordingly. This can be computationally expensive for large datasets but usually provides stable convergence.

Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD): In SGD, the gradient is computed using a single data point or sample, randomly picked from the dataset. This leads to faster computation times but noisier updates, which can sometimes result in a less stable convergence.

Mini-batch Gradient Descent: This is a compromise between batch gradient descent and SGD. The gradient is computed using a small subset or batch of the dataset, typically containing 10 to 1000 samples. This provides a balance between computational efficiency and convergence stability.

slides.title: Self-Learning
bullets: Hyperparameter Tuning with Grid Search using Cross Validation & Bootstrapping

slides.title: Hyperparameters
bullets: Each machine learning model needs a training, which is a process of tuning their hyperparameters and selecting their features (variables) for the best predictive performance.
For example, k in kNN, the number of hidden layers in Neural Networks, even the degree of of polynomials in a linear regression have to be tuned.
This tuning process is the core idea in self-learning algorithms.

slides.title:
Suppose Model1 requires to pick a value for λ, perhaps it is a degree of polynomials in the model.
We establish a grid, a set of sequential numbers, that is a set of possible values of λ.
We split the data into k random sections, let’s say 10 proportionally equal sections.
We leave one section out and use 9 sections. The combination of these 9 sections is our training set. The one that is left out is our validation set.
We fit the model using each value in the set of possible values of λ. For example, if we have 100 values of λ, we fit the model to the training set 100 times, once for each possible value of λ.
We evaluate each of 100 models by using their predictive accuracy on the validation set, the one that is left out. We pick a λ that gives the highest prediction accuracy.
We do this process 10 times (if it is 10-fold cross validation) with each time using a different section of the data as the validation set. So, note that each time our training and validation sets are going to be different. At the end, in total we will have 10 best λs.
We pick the average or modal value of λ as our optimal hyperparameter that tunes our predictive model for its best performance.

slides.title: Picking a  metricdepends onthe context
bullets: Medical Diagnosis (Emphasizing Sensitivity):
Consider a test for a serious but treatable disease like cancer.

You would want the test to correctly identifies as many patients as possible who actually have the disease. Missing a true case (a false negative) could be life-threatening, whereas a false positive (identifying someone as having the disease when they don't) is less dangerous as it can be clarified with further testing.
Spam Email Filtering (Emphasizing Precision):
Consider an email system that filters out spam messages.
High precision means that most of the emails marked as spam are indeed spam, and very few legitimate emails are incorrectly filtered out.

slides.title: Pick your + class
bullets: You are developing a model to detect fraudulent transactions for a bank. There are two classes: "Fraudulent" and "Non-Fraudulent".

If you choose "Fraudulent" as the positive class, the focus of your metrics will be on correctly identifying fraudulent transactions. Metrics like Sensitivity (True Positive Rate) will indicate how good your model is at catching frauds, which is the primary goal in this context.

If "Non-Fraudulent" is chosen as the positive class, the metrics would then emphasize the correct identification of legitimate transactions. While this is also important, it might not be the primary goal in a fraud detection context.
TPR vs. FPR
TPR = 1 but FPR = 1
FPR = 0 but TPR = 0

slides.title: Pearl Harbor – WW2
bullets: Radar Receiver Operator had to predict which aircraft is Japanese from the radar signals
You can call all aircrafts are Japanese, but then you take yours down too

Following the attack on Pearl Harbor in 1941, the United States army began new research to increase the prediction of correctly detected Japanese aircraft from their radar signals. For these purposes they measured the ability of a radar receiver operator to make these important distinctions, which was called the Receiver Operating Characteristic.

slides.title: ROC - Receiver Operating Characteristics Curve
bullets: An ROC curve (receiver operating characteristic curve) is a graph showing the performance of a classification model at all classification thresholds. This curve plots two parameters: TPR and FPR

slides.title: AUC - Area Under the Curve
bullets: AUC measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve (think integral calculus) from (0,0) to (1,1)
The AUC represents the probability that a randomly chosen positive instance (Digit 2) is ranked higher by the classifier than a randomly chosen negative instance (Digit 7).

slides.title: Model Selection by AUC, then what?
bullets: AUC is good for tuning the models and selecting the best among them
It avoids finding the optimal discriminating threshold
But after finding the best tuned model, we still need Sensitivity, Recall, F1 Score … Hence, we need to find the confusion table
But … the Confusion Table is the results of the optimal threshold
How are we going to the optimal threshold?

slides.title: Youden’s Index (J-Index)
bullets: The best threshold is often considered to be the one that is furthest away from the line of no-discrimination (which would be the diagonal line from the bottom left to the top right in an ROC plot).
This point maximizes both sensitivity (true positive rate) and specificity ( 1 - false positive rate) for the classifier.
A common metric used to find the best threshold is the Youden’s J statistic.
Youden’s index is defined as: J = True Positive Rate (TPR) + True Negative Rate (TNR) -1
Since, FPR = 1-TNR, J = TPR – FPR
So J will be maximized when Marginal TPR = MFPR
